working with powers of tens, as in this example, should be straightforward to special-case

m.new_content.body: working with powers of ten, as in this example (and other similar conversions), should be straightforward to special-case

content.body: I do not think we should special-case powers of ten. The spec already has full real-number arithmetic, and translating to binary64 is just inherently lossy—we either do the latter only after the former, or bite the bullet on having surprises like this (but for my part at least, I was shocked to see that `5 * 1e-6 !== 5 / 1e-6`).
content.formatted_body: I do not think we should special-case powers of ten. The slide talking about this as a "known 'issue'" is the right thing to do; as noted in the issue, this is "just now Numbers work" and is the result of the highly intentional move to use float math instead of decimal math that we did to address the V8 concerns.
Do we already have real-number arithmetic on non-integer values?

yes: https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/notational-conventions.html#sec-mathematical-operations
> *Mathematical values*: Arbitrary real numbers, used as the default numeric type.
> *Extended mathematical values*: Mathematical values together with +∞ and -∞.
> Numeric operators such as +, ×, =, and ≥ refer to those operations as determined by the type of the operands. When applied to mathematical values, the operators refer to the usual mathematical operations. When applied to extended mathematical values, the operators refer to the usual mathematical operations over the extended real numbers; indeterminate forms are not defined and their use in this specification should be considered an editorial error.

content.body: Where and how do we define the _mathematical value_ of a non-integral finite Number? I'm not finding it in ECMA-262. The [Number Type](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-ecmascript-language-types-number-type) section gets really close, but it does not actually say that where it says "value" that's the same as "mathematical value".
content.formatted_body: Where and how do we define the mathematical value of a non-integral finite Number? I'm not finding it in ECMA-262. The Number Type section gets really close, but it does not actually say that where it says "value" that's the same as "mathematical value".

content.body: Intl stringifies and parses. I don't know if there is a standalone float-to-MV AO.
It's important to define the semantics of the conversion. Stringify-and-parse is a well-defined semantic (and not the only such semantic).

I agree, and I'm going to file a PR fixing [this line](https://github.com/tc39/proposal-amount/blob/643ba440bb86065ea42ea3b2def903c7b1f7f1b1/spec.emu#L457) in the Amount proposal.

My question is more generic, though, given that the spec explicitly [says this](https://tc39.es/ecma262/#sec-mathematical-operations):
> A conversion from a Number or BigInt x to a mathematical value is denoted as "the mathematical value of x", or ℝ(x). The mathematical value of +0𝔽 and -0𝔽 is the mathematical value 0. The mathematical value of non-finite values is not defined.

As we say that, I definitely expect that mathematical values of finite Number values are defined _somewhere_. And that "value" and "mathematical value" are not interchangeable.
content.formatted_body:
I agree, and I'm going to file a PR fixing this line in the Amount proposal.

My question is more generic, though, given that the spec explicitly says this:

A conversion from a Number or BigInt x to a mathematical value is denoted as "the mathematical value of x", or ℝ(x). The mathematical value of +0𝔽 and -0𝔽 is the mathematical value 0. The mathematical value of non-finite values is not defined.

As we say that, I definitely expect that mathematical values of finite Number values are defined somewhere. And that "value" and "mathematical value" are not interchangeable.

content.body: https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/notational-conventions.html#mathematical-value-of

* https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/notational-conventions.html#mathematical-value-of
> A conversion from a Number or BigInt *x* to a mathematical value is denoted as "the *mathematical value of* *x*", or ℝ(*x*). The mathematical value of **+0**𝔽 and **-0**𝔽 is the mathematical value 0. The mathematical value of non-finite values is not defined. The *extended mathematical value of* *x* is the mathematical value of *x* for finite values, and is +∞ and -∞ for **+∞**𝔽 and **-∞**𝔽 respectively; it is not defined for **NaN**.
content.formatted_body:
*

https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/notational-conventions.html#mathematical-value-of

A conversion from a Number or BigInt x to a mathematical value is denoted as "the mathematical value of x", or ℝ(x). The mathematical value of +0𝔽 and -0𝔽 is the mathematical value 0. The mathematical value of non-finite values is not defined. The extended mathematical value of x is the mathematical value of x for finite values, and is +∞ and -∞ for **+∞**𝔽 and **-∞**𝔽 respectively; it is not defined for NaN.

content.body: so every finite Number is exactly equal to (-1)**_sign_ × _significand_ × 2 ** (_E_ - 1023), with bit-size bounds on _significand_ and _E_

content.body: * so every finite Number is exactly equal to (-1)\*\*_sign_ × _significand_ × 2\*\*(_E_ - 1023), with bit-size bounds on _significand_ and _E_ (the latter of which is an 11-bit unsigned integer, and the former of which can be derived from a 52-bit unsigned integer)
content.formatted_body: * so every finite Number is exactly equal to (-1)**sign × significand × 2**(E - 1023), with bit-size bounds on significand and E (the latter of which is an 11-bit unsigned integer, and the former of which can be derived from a 52-bit unsigned integer)

content.body: Note that for BigInts there's no such issue, as they're explicitly described as "representing arbitrary integers in a one-to-one correspondence".

String(_number_) goes to [Number::toString](https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/ecmascript-data-types-and-values.html#sec-numeric-types-number-tostring), which doesn't convert _number_ to a mathematical value (rather, it constructs mathematical integers that can be manipulated to tightly bracket _number_)
content.formatted_body: String(number) goes to Number::toString, which doesn't convert number to a mathematical value (rather, it constructs mathematical integers that can be manipulated to tightly bracket number)

content.body: Amount PR fixing the issue: https://github.com/tc39/proposal-amount/pull/112
